content.claim: Selective tracking schools raise short-term achievement scores of admitted students but produce a deteriorating effect on test scores of students left in non-selective schools; however, tracking's impact on individual long-run labor-market outcomes appears to disappear by adulthood, suggesting short-term gains may be partly zero-sum.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: Based on single empirical strand not multiply replicated internationally
Relies on CEU thesis and Horn 2013 analysis, limited replication
Short-term vs. long-run findings derive from different methodological approaches
Labor-market outcome analysis timeline not specified in available documentation
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
